author: @daveh6356
text: I 're-invented' one of these on my 3-hour road trips to my favourite kayaking spot in Rotorua. No prompts, avoided reading about it, just started with angled cones but ended up with three tilted hemisphere's. Gutted to find someone (Honda I think) had a production-ish item already but a great thought exercise. Shame we had no 3D printers back then.

author: @russellzauner
text: Now I want to understand how the automatic CVT function works - I can set a level of constant torque where it automatically adjusts to keep it where set regardless of the terrain or pedal speed.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's not automatic mate - you have to shift the lever - but making it automatic is not going to be really different from a standard automatic gearbox in current use would be my guess

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Enviolo/NuVinci has something they call the "Automatiq" system that "shifts" for you (such as it is) to maintain a constant effort level from you.  I have the manual system; the upgrade is pretty expensive to me so haven't really gone for it yet (over 2x the cost of the hub alone and it also requires its own small battery).  I see some literature/drawings/pictures from them but I still don't really quite understand it.

replies.author: @russellzauner
replies.text: also thanks for replying!  😀  have a wonderful day!

author: @salimufari
text: While the principals are sound I see limited uses. Friction means either wear on the mating surfaces or heat on parts causing expansion & potential seizure of the bearings in their raceways. As long as wear & heat are managed this looks fantastic & fun.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well it is a model of an actual device that is actually in use - so there is research to answer your questions. But you are right - good points and it is fun for sure

author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Cool.
Please, could you put some markers on the input and output, as it is a bit hard to see them turning?
Thanks.

Too many years ago, I tried to make an automatic gearbox for a RC car, using a 'cones and ball-bearings' approach. It sort of worked, after so many, 'trial and error' prototypes. But I never cracked the tendency of it to jump from 'low' to 'high' ratio when under high torque, or at high revs (regardless of adjusting spring tension, and various curves/angles of the 'cones') it would jump and stick at low or high ratio. And one of the hardest things for me to do was accurately mount the small ball bearings (5-7mm). The latter problem caused all manner of vibration and spacing issues. It was both fun and very frustrating. But I learned a lot about the difference between 'seems like a good idea' and the engineering reality. And it would slip either too much and/or unpredictably given the slightest excuse, even an errant fingerprint could cause problems.
I digress.

Thanks again for showing us all these fascinating techniques a mechanisms.
ATB. 👍 🫡

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are markers on there mate - reflective dots - I agree there is a world of difference between the idea of a thing and the reality of a thing lol - cheers mate

replies.author: @bikerfirefarter7280
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering 
Sorry, watching on a dodgy/tiny mobile screen, I couldn't make them out. My bad.  🙂

Thanks.

author: @IvoTichelaar
text: I have a nuvinci gearhub on my cargo bike. I tested the bike with normal gears and a derailleur, and the bike felt very efficient and rideable without electric assist (necessary to know as the bike is relatively heavy and one does end up with an empty battery occasionally). The nuvinci hub is clearly not as efficient and running out of battery range makes for a more difficult ride home. However, I find riding on a bicycle with regular discrete gears extremely clunky know. I have developed a pretty good feel for how much up or down I need to shift to change gear with the right amount, and you can do that while pedaling on. It is very comfortable to always hit that perfect gear ratio. At first I was disappointed with the lower efficiency of the nuvinci, but by now I would definitely want one on any bicycle that I would buy in the future.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice feedback mate - i heard nu-vinci were working with a friction fluid addition to their drive to improve efficiency

author: @briancooke4259
text: That is amazing!  Thank you for sharing this with us!  If the plates that applied pressure were directly linked with springs, wouldn't that eliminate the need for adjustment?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't think so mate - the springs do two things - the first is to apply a normal force and your suggestion would be spot on for that. But they also centre the spheres in the holder so they don't rub on the holder surfaces - I don't think your suggestion would help there - but there is always more than one way to do a job - so another method to do that coupled with your suggestion would do that

replies.author: @briancooke4259
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringthat makes perfect sense, thank you for the correction!  Can I ask, is the swashplate CVT a kind of nutation drive?

author: @Bill-n9l7d
text: I think I'm starting to get what you are getting at remember 42 times....😊 I'll get there. I often wonder why some auto companies don't do straight shifts with a high&low speed rear end like an 18 wheeler you'd have ten gears and you'd only need a very small engine. It just always made sense to me. You'd just need to make either the three or four cylinder engines with smaller pistons depending on the demand put on the car. I think people would like them.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they probably would in this day and age mate - they would be economical

replies.author: @Bill-n9l7d
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkeringyes they would be very economical as well as an effective way to reduce Petro dependence. However I just recently talked to a retired trucker and he says the companies are replacing the manual gear boxes with automatic transmission because they can't find drivers. Which means the transfer truck makers have figured out how to bring the rear end into the realm of automatic. So in a car it would still give better mileage and how much I don't know. Maybe our chat will inspire someone who makes automobiles we can hope

author: @MrNatural451
text:
Now, THAT  was cool! When you mentioned that the balls could be controlled with a lever, I immediately imagined all kinds of (mad scientist) devices where the speed is controlled by a large steampunk lever. Nice little CVT!
 But you've gone back to all white contructions. They look great, but can be hard follow in a video. May I humbly suggest printing some parts in a light grey. This would make it easier for us at home to see it better while maintaining the aesthetics.
Please take this as it's meant. A friendly suggestion.
Love your videos, no matter what.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

replies.author: @Kineth1
replies.text: Automobiles have a lot of power available and very neglectful operators.  The reaction of most personal automobile operators to "it won't go" is "give it more power", not "find out why it won't go".  These need something in control that will protect them.

replies.author: @IvoTichelaar
replies.text: An electric motor as used in cars has enough power and torque throughout its range of speeds to not need a gearbox. There are a few with maybe two gears, to lower engine noise at high speeds. But overall it's an unnecessary addition to an EV. And EVs are heavy enough, and gear boxes wear, so best to design them without it.
